front: Law: secret, largely religiously-tolerant law code kept by this empire
Institution: tribal assembly that elected new khans, seating a leader on white felt and giving him a golden sword
Figure: wrestling princess, daughter of Kaidu, who defeated thousands of suitors in matches and dragged enemy soldiers off their horses in battle
Concept: ritual pact of sworn brotherhood between two Mongol men, sealed with an exchange of gifts like a black sable coat
Concept: Mongol clan structure dividing relatives into "black bone," among whom marriage was allowed, and "white bone," among whom it was not
Ruler: born with the name Temujin, allegedly clutching a blood clot in his fist at birth
work: Genghis Khan and the Unification of the Mongols

front: Ruler: his wife Borte was kidnapped by the Merkit tribe and rescued by him with the help of his blood-brother Jamukha
work: Genghis Khan and the Unification of the Mongols

front: Ruler: inflicted a bloodless death by strangulation on his former blood-brother Jamukha after subduing the Naimans and Merkits
work: Genghis Khan and the Unification of the Mongols

front: Institution: elite bodyguard created by Genghis Khan, led by the sons of his regiment commanders
work: Genghis Khan and the Unification of the Mongols

front: Institution: Mongol army division created by Genghis Khan, organized into units of 10,000 soldiers
work: Genghis Khan and the Unification of the Mongols

front: Institution: countrywide relay-messenger system established by Genghis Khan, whose riders carried seal-passes called paiza
work: Genghis Khan and the Unification of the Mongols

front: Ruler: had Tata-Tonga, a captured member of the Naiman tribe, create a writing script for the Mongols
work: Genghis Khan and the Unification of the Mongols

front: Document: discovered in the 19th century by the Russian Archimandrite Palladius, who produced a partial translation
back: The Secret History of the Mongols
work: The Secret History of the Mongols

front: Document: opens with a genealogy in which the ancestress Alan Gua is impregnated by a "glittering spirit," descending from a blue-gray wolf and a red doe
back: The Secret History of the Mongols
work: The Secret History of the Mongols

front: Document: shares its title with a work by the Byzantine historian Procopius
back: The Secret History of the Mongols
work: The Secret History of the Mongols

front: Document: its narrative ends in the reign of Ogedei Khan
back: The Secret History of the Mongols
work: The Secret History of the Mongols

front: Event: opened with a slaughter of Jin troops at the Badger's Mouth pass
work: The Mongol Conquest of Jin and Song China

front: Event: combatants deployed counterweight trebuchets nicknamed "Muslim Paos" and used iron chains to lower gunpowder "thunder-crash bombs"
work: The Mongol Conquest of Jin and Song China

front: Event: the Toluid Civil War, between Kublai Khan and his brother Ariq Boke, delayed this campaign against the Song for three years
work: The Mongol Conquest of Jin and Song China

front: Event: Ogedei Khan oversaw the final fall of the Jin dynasty during its early stage
work: The Mongol Conquest of Jin and Song China

front: Event: the Song navy, despite a ten-to-one numerical advantage, was destroyed at the 1279 Battle of Yamen, ending this campaign
work: The Mongol Conquest of Jin and Song China

front: Dynasty: founded by Kublai Khan, ruling China from capitals at Shangdu ("Xanadu") and Khanbaliq
Dynasty: issued paper currency called chao, made from mulberry-tree bark
Dynasty: commissioned the 'Phags-pa script as a universal writing system for Chinese and Tibetan
Dynasty: hosted zaju theater, including Guan Hanqing's play The Injustice to Dou E
Dynasty: was visited by the Venetian traveler Marco Polo, whose account was recorded by Rustichello da Pisa
Dynasty: employed the corrupt finance minister Ahmad Fanakati
Dynasty: saw the Ispah rebellion at its port of Zaytun, origin of the English word "satin"
Event: overthrew the Yuan Dynasty in 1368, led by the future Hongwu Emperor Zhu Yuanzhang and named for its participants' trademark headwear
Event: a remnant of this dynasty in Mongolia was restored in the 15th century under Queen Mandukhai the Wise and Dayan Khan
Event: both of Kublai Khan's invasions of this country began with battles fought on Tsushima Island
Event: both Mongol invasion fleets were destroyed by typhoons the Japanese called kamikaze, or "divine wind"
Document: scroll commissioned by the warrior Takezaki Suenaga to glorify his own disobedient bravery at the Battle of Bun'ei
Concept: Japanese school of swordsmithing that emerged by studying blades broken during the Mongol invasions
Event: left the Kamakura shogunate unable to reward its gokenin vassals with conquered land, weakening the shogunate
Event: launched by Batu Khan in 1241 on the pretext of pursuing the Cuman leader Koten, who had taken refuge with Hungary's King Bela IV
Battle: Duke Henry the Pious of Silesia was killed and his head paraded on a spike after this engagement
back: Battle of Legnica (Mongol Invasion of Europe)

front: Battle: nine sacks were filled with the right ears of slain Polish knights after this engagement, an event still commemorated by Krakow's Lajkonik festival
back: Battle of Legnica (Mongol Invasion of Europe)

front: Battle: the general Subotai lured Hungarian forces into a marshy trap and annihilated them at this engagement, fought near the Sajo river
back: Battle of Mohi (Mongol Invasion of Europe)

front: Battle: King Bela IV of Hungary weakened his position before this engagement by expelling the Cuman people from his realm
back: Battle of Mohi (Mongol Invasion of Europe)

front: Event: ended only when the 1241 death of Great Khan Ogedei forced a return east for a kurultai to choose his successor
Empire: founded by Batu Khan, grandson of Genghis Khan; its name supposedly derives from the color of its shining tents
Empire: ruled from its capital at Sarai, issuing patents of authority over Russian princes called yarlyks
Figure: continued to pay tribute to the Golden Horde even after defeating the Swedes at the Neva and the Teutonic Knights at the Battle of the Ice
Battle: Dmitri Donskoi's 1380 victory here began to diminish the Golden Horde's hegemony over Russia
Document: epic that recounts the Golden Horde's defeat at the Battle of Kulikovo
Event: Ivan III decisively broke the Golden Horde's power by forcing its retreat here in 1480
back: Great Stand on the Ugra (Golden Horde)

front: Empire: Persia-based khanate founded by Hulagu Khan south of the Golden Horde; its name ("il-khan") denotes subordination to the Great Khan
Event: Hulagu Khan dismantled this Assassin stronghold before sacking Baghdad
Event: this 1258 sack ended the Abbasid Caliphate; the Tigris allegedly ran black with ink from the destroyed House of Wisdom
Figure: this Christian Khereid princess, Hulagu Khan's wife, persuaded him to spare Baghdad's Christians during its sack
Battle: the Mamluks under Baibars defeated Ilkhanate forces here in 1260, halting the Mongol advance toward Cairo
Event: proposals for this Ilkhanate alliance against the Mamluks, drawn up at the Second Council of Lyons, fell through for lack of royal support
Ruler: later Ilkhan who converted to Islam
Ruler: the Ilkhanate's final ruler, who may have died of the Black Death
People: branch of the Oirat Mongols who ruled a namesake khanate based in present-day Xinjiang
work: The Dzungars and the Qing Conquest

front: Figure: this Dzungar queen died leading a cavalry charge to cover her husband Galdan Khan's escape from the Kangxi Emperor's forces
work: The Dzungars and the Qing Conquest

front: Ruler: this Dzungar leader was killed after losing the Battle of Jao Modo to the Kangxi Emperor while trying to conquer the Khalkha Mongols
work: The Dzungars and the Qing Conquest

front: Event: the first three of the Qianlong Emperor's Ten Great Campaigns targeted the Dzungars in this genocide, which killed roughly 80 percent of their population
work: The Dzungars and the Qing Conquest

front: Figure: Dzungar leader whose rebellion against Qing forces was crushed in the second of the Ten Great Campaigns
work: The Dzungars and the Qing Conquest

front: Event: this country declared independence from the Qing dynasty in 1911, first ruled as the Bogd Khanate
Figure: nicknamed the "Mad Baron," he restored the Bogd Khan to power in 1921 with a White Russian force before being toppled by the Red Army
Event: this country's capital, Niislel Khuree, was renamed Ulaanbaatar ("Red Hero") in 1924
Event: as a Soviet satellite, this country's troops fought Japan at the 1939 Battle of Khalkhin Gol
Figure: known as the "Stalin of Mongolia," he led Stalinist purges that suppressed Buddhist institutions and hoped to reunite Outer and Inner Mongolia
Event: peaceful revolution in 1990 that ended Communist rule, centered on Ulaanbaatar's Sukhbaatar Square
Event: mass protests broke out in this country in 2022 after an estimated $13 billion in coal was stolen from state reserves
Concept: sky god at the center of traditional Mongol shamanism
Concept: stone-and-wood cairn shrines revered in traditional Mongol religion
Concept: traditional Mongol pantheon of 99 sky spirits, divided into "white" and "black" groups and presided over by a sky god in the form of a goose
Concept: this Tibetan Buddhist title for a religious teacher originates from the Mongol language
Concept: annual festival featuring the "Three Manly Skills" of wrestling, horse racing, and archery
Concept: overtone vocal technique also practiced in the neighboring republic of Tuva
Concept: two-stringed bowed instrument traditionally topped with a carved horse head
Concept: portable, felt-covered tent dwelling erected near good pastureland
